---
title: "Introduction to Machine Learning for Marketing"
subtitle: "Week 1 • Applied Machine Learning with tidymodels"
author: "Jae Jung"
institute: "IBM 6540, Cal Poly Pomona"
date: today
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: [default, cpp-brand.scss]
    footer: "Week 1: Introduction to Machine Learning"
    logo: images/cpp_primary_2c_green_cmyk.png
    transition: concave
    transition-speed: fast
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 1
    toc-title: "Table of Contents"
    number-sections: false
    slide-number: true
    preview-links: auto
    incremental: false
    code-line-numbers: true
    code-copy: true
    code-fold: false
    code-overflow: wrap
    smaller: false
    embed-resources: true
execute:
  echo: true
  warning: false
  message: false
editor: visual
---

# What is Machine Learning? {background-color="#FFB81C"}

## The core idea

> Machine learning is a set of methods that allow computers to **learn patterns from data** and use those patterns to make predictions or decisions on new, unseen cases.

Unlike traditional programming:

| Traditional programming        | Machine learning                     |
|--------------------------------|--------------------------------------|
| You write explicit rules       | The algorithm learns rules from data |
| Rules → Output                 | Data + Output → Rules                |
| Fragile when rules are complex | Scales well to complex patterns      |

::: notes
Start with the intuition. Students understand rules-based logic. The flip is the key insight.
:::

## Why does this matter for marketers?

Marketing decisions are **prediction problems** in disguise:

::::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Who should we target?**

- Which customers will respond to an offer?
- Which leads will convert?
- Which subscribers will churn?
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
**What should we do?**

- Which product to recommend?
- Which message to send?
- What price to set?
:::
:::::

::: callout-tip
Every time you ask *"which customers are most likely to..."*, you are describing a machine learning problem.
:::

## Prediction vs. explanation

These are related but **different goals**:

| Goal | Question | Example |
|----|----|----|
| **Explanation** | Why does X happen? | Does ad spend *cause* sales? |
| **Prediction** | What will happen next? | Which customers will churn *next month*? |

::: fragment
- **Statistics** traditionally focuses on explanation — coefficients, significance, causality
- **Machine learning** traditionally focuses on prediction — accuracy, generalization, new data
:::

::: callout-note
In this course, we focus on **prediction**. The same model (e.g. linear regression) can serve both goals — the goal shapes how we use it.
:::

## TMWR Chapter 1 — Key ideas

From *Tidy Modeling with R*, Chapter 1:

- Models are tools for **understanding data** and **making decisions**
- The modeling process is iterative: explore → build → evaluate → refine
- Software shapes how you think about modeling — tidymodels encourages good habits

::: callout-important
A model is only as good as the **question it is answering** and the **data it is trained on**.
:::

::: fragment
Before writing a single line of code, ask:

1.  What is the outcome I am trying to predict?
2.  Who will use this prediction, and how?
3.  What data do I have, and is it appropriate?
:::

# Supervised Learning {background-color="#FFB81C"}

## What is supervised learning?

**Supervised learning** uses labeled data — data where the outcome is already known — to train a model that can predict that outcome for new cases.

::::: columns
::: {.column width="55%"}
**The setup:**

- You have a dataset with inputs (features) and a known output (label)
- You train a model on this data
- You evaluate how well it predicts on new, unseen data
:::

::: {.column width="45%"}
**Marketing examples:**

- Past campaigns with known response rates
- Customer records with known churn status
- Orders with known revenue amounts
:::
:::::

::: callout-tip
"Supervised" means the algorithm is learning from **examples with correct answers**.
:::

## Two types of supervised learning

::::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}
### Regression

Predicts a **continuous numeric** outcome.

**Examples:** - Customer lifetime value (\$) - Predicted spending amount - Weekly sales revenue - Satisfaction score (1–10) - Time until next purchase (days)

**Key models:** `linear_reg()` • `rand_forest()` • `boost_tree()`
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
### Classification

Predicts a **category or class**.

**Examples:** - Will the customer churn? (yes/no) - Will a lead convert? (yes/no) - Is this review positive or negative? - Which segment does this customer belong to?

**Key models:** `logistic_reg()` • `rand_forest()` • `boost_tree()`
:::
:::::

## Regression — deeper look

Regression answers: **"How much?"** or **"How many?"**

``` r
# Predict customer lifetime value
lm_spec <- linear_reg() |>
  set_engine("lm")

lm_fit <- lm_spec |>
  fit(lifetime_value ~ recency + frequency + monetary + tenure,
      data = customer_train)
```

**Common evaluation metrics:**

| Metric | What it measures                                    |
|--------|-----------------------------------------------------|
| RMSE   | Average prediction error (same units as outcome)    |
| MAE    | Average absolute error (less sensitive to outliers) |
| R²     | Proportion of variance explained (0 to 1)           |

## Classification — deeper look

Classification answers: **"Which category?"** or **"Will it happen?"**

``` r
# Predict churn (yes/no)
lr_spec <- logistic_reg() |>
  set_engine("glm")

lr_fit <- lr_spec |>
  fit(churn ~ recency + num_complaints + tenure + last_purchase_days,
      data = customer_train)
```

**Common evaluation metrics:**

| Metric               | What it measures                            |
|----------------------|---------------------------------------------|
| Accuracy             | \% of predictions correct                   |
| Sensitivity (Recall) | \% of actual positives caught               |
| Specificity          | \% of actual negatives correctly identified |
| ROC AUC              | Overall discrimination ability (0.5 to 1.0) |

## Supervised learning algorithms

::::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}
### Linear regression

- Predicts numeric outcomes
- Fast, interpretable
- Assumes linear relationships
- Good baseline model

### Logistic regression

- Predicts binary outcomes
- Outputs probabilities
- Interpretable coefficients
- Great starting point for classification
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
### Decision trees

- Splits data using if/then rules
- Highly interpretable
- Can overfit if too deep
- Foundation for ensemble methods

### Support Vector Machines (SVM)

- Finds optimal decision boundary
- Works well in high dimensions
- Less interpretable
- Good for text classification
:::
:::::

## Ensemble methods

**Random forests** and **gradient boosting** combine many weak models into one strong model:

::::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}
### Random Forest

- Builds many decision trees
- Each tree sees a random sample of data and features
- Averages predictions across trees
- Reduces overfitting dramatically
- `rand_forest()` in tidymodels
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
### Gradient Boosting

- Builds trees **sequentially**
- Each tree corrects the errors of the previous one
- Often highest accuracy in practice
- Requires more tuning
- `boost_tree()` in tidymodels
:::
:::::

::: callout-tip
For most marketing prediction problems, **random forests** and **gradient boosting** are strong default choices.
:::

## Neural networks

Neural networks are loosely inspired by the human brain:

- Layers of interconnected nodes learn complex, non-linear patterns
- Very powerful for images, text, and large datasets
- Require more data and compute than traditional models
- Less interpretable ("black box")
- In tidymodels: `mlp()` (multilayer perceptron)

::: callout-note
For most **tabular marketing data** (spreadsheet-style), tree-based models often perform comparably to neural networks with far less complexity. Neural networks shine with images, text, and very large datasets.
:::

# Unsupervised Learning {background-color="#FFB81C"}

## What is unsupervised learning?

**Unsupervised learning** works with data that has **no labeled outcome**. The algorithm finds structure, patterns, or groupings on its own.

::::: columns
::: {.column width="55%"}
**The setup:**

- You have inputs (features) but no known output
- The algorithm discovers patterns in the data
- You interpret what the patterns mean
:::

::: {.column width="45%"}
**Marketing examples:**

- Customer segmentation
- Product grouping
- Review theme discovery
- Survey dimension reduction
:::
:::::

::: callout-tip
"Unsupervised" means no correct answers — the algorithm explores the data and you interpret what it finds.
:::

## Clustering

Clustering groups observations that are **similar to each other** and **different from other groups**.

**Marketing applications:**

| Use case | What you cluster | What you get |
|----|----|----|
| Customer segmentation | RFM features, demographics | Actionable customer groups |
| Product grouping | Purchase patterns, attributes | Product families |
| Store segmentation | Sales patterns, location data | Store archetypes |

::: fragment
**Key question:** How many clusters?

This is one of the hardest decisions in clustering. We use methods like the elbow plot and silhouette scores to guide the choice.
:::

## K-Means clustering

K-Means is the most widely used clustering algorithm:

1.  You specify **k** (number of clusters)
2.  Algorithm assigns each observation to the nearest cluster center
3.  Centers are recalculated
4.  Steps 2–3 repeat until stable

``` r
library(tidymodels)
library(tidyclust)

kmeans_spec <- k_means(num_clusters = 4)

kmeans_fit <- kmeans_spec |>
  fit(~ recency + frequency + monetary, data = customer_data)

customer_data |>
  bind_cols(predict(kmeans_fit, customer_data))
```

## Hierarchical clustering

Hierarchical clustering builds a **tree of clusters** (dendrogram):

::::: columns
::: {.column width="55%"}
**How it works:**

- **Agglomerative** (bottom-up): Start with each observation as its own cluster, then merge the most similar pairs
- **Divisive** (top-down): Start with one cluster, then split

**Advantages:** - No need to pre-specify k - Visual dendrogram shows structure - Can cut the tree at any level
:::

::: {.column width="45%"}
**Marketing use:**

- Understand natural customer hierarchy
- Explore data before choosing k
- Useful for smaller datasets

**Linkage methods:** - Complete linkage - Average linkage - Ward's method (most common)
:::
:::::

## DBSCAN

DBSCAN (Density-Based Spatial Clustering of Applications with Noise) finds clusters of **arbitrary shape**:

- Groups points that are **closely packed together**
- Points in low-density regions are labeled as **noise/outliers**
- Does not require specifying k in advance

::::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Advantages:** - Finds non-spherical clusters - Handles outliers naturally - Does not require k
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
**Disadvantages:** - Requires tuning two parameters (epsilon, min_samples) - Struggles with varying density - Less common in tidymodels
:::
:::::

::: callout-note
For most **marketing segmentation** tasks, k-means is the practical starting point. DBSCAN is useful when you suspect non-spherical clusters or want to identify anomalous customers.
:::

## Dimensionality reduction

Real marketing datasets often have **many features**. Dimensionality reduction compresses them into fewer, more meaningful dimensions.

**Principal Component Analysis (PCA):**

- Finds linear combinations of features that capture the most variance
- Reduces many correlated variables into a few uncorrelated components
- Common in marketing survey analysis and feature engineering

``` r
pca_recipe <- recipe(~ ., data = survey_data) |>
  step_normalize(all_numeric_predictors()) |>
  step_pca(all_numeric_predictors(), num_comp = 3)

pca_prep <- prep(pca_recipe)
pca_baked <- bake(pca_prep, new_data = NULL)
```

## Supervised vs. unsupervised — summary

|   | Supervised | Unsupervised |
|----|----|----|
| **Labeled data needed?** | Yes | No |
| **Goal** | Predict known outcome | Discover structure |
| **Evaluation** | Accuracy, RMSE, AUC | Interpretability, cohesion |
| **Marketing examples** | Churn, CLV, conversion | Segments, themes, dimensions |
| **tidymodels tools** | `parsnip`, `workflows` | `tidyclust`, `recipes` |

# Tidymodels {background-color="#FFB81C"}

## What is tidymodels?

**tidymodels** is a collection of R packages for modeling and machine learning that follow **tidy data principles**:

- Consistent, predictable syntax across all model types
- Integrates with the tidyverse (`dplyr`, `ggplot2`, `tibble`)
- Separates modeling steps into clear, reusable components
- Makes it easy to compare and switch between models

::: callout-tip
Just as the tidyverse unified data wrangling, **tidymodels unifies the modeling workflow**.
:::

Reference: <https://www.tidymodels.org/>

## The tidymodels ecosystem

| Workflow step        | Package     | What it does                              |
|----------------------|-------------|-------------------------------------------|
| Split data           | `rsample`   | Training/testing splits, cross-validation |
| Preprocess features  | `recipes`   | Feature engineering, scaling, encoding    |
| Specify models       | `parsnip`   | Unified model interface across engines    |
| Combine steps        | `workflows` | Bundle recipe + model together            |
| Evaluate performance | `yardstick` | Accuracy, RMSE, AUC, and more             |
| Tune hyperparameters | `tune`      | Grid search, Bayesian optimization        |
| Tidy model outputs   | `broom`     | Clean model summaries as tibbles          |

## TMWR Chapter 3 — A review of R modeling fundamentals

From *Tidy Modeling with R*, Chapter 3:

- R has many modeling frameworks — `lm()`, `glm()`, `caret`, `mlr3` — each with different syntax
- tidymodels provides a **unified interface** so you write code the same way regardless of the underlying algorithm
- The **formula interface** (`outcome ~ predictor1 + predictor2`) is central to R modeling

``` r
# Base R
lm(sales ~ ad_spend + price, data = train_data)

# tidymodels (same model, consistent syntax)
linear_reg() |>
  set_engine("lm") |>
  fit(sales ~ ad_spend + price, data = train_data)
```

## TMWR Chapter 5 — Spending our data budget

From *Tidy Modeling with R*, Chapter 5:

> We only have so much data. Every time we use data to make a modeling decision, we "spend" some of its ability to give us unbiased estimates.

**The data budget:**

::::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}
### Training set (\~80%)

- Used to fit the model
- Used for feature engineering decisions
- Used for hyperparameter tuning
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
### Testing set (\~20%)

- Used **once** at the very end
- Estimates real-world performance
- Never used to make modeling decisions
:::
:::::

::: callout-warning
Using the test set more than once leads to **optimistic, misleading** performance estimates.
:::

## The initial split

``` r
library(tidymodels)

set.seed(123)  # for reproducibility

# Split 80% training, 20% testing
data_split <- initial_split(customer_data, prop = 0.80)

# Extract training and testing sets
train_data <- training(data_split)
test_data  <- testing(data_split)

# Check sizes
nrow(train_data)  # ~80% of rows
nrow(test_data)   # ~20% of rows
```

::: callout-tip
Always set a **random seed** with `set.seed()` before splitting so your results are reproducible.
:::

## Stratified splitting

When your outcome is **imbalanced** (e.g. only 5% churn), use stratified splitting to ensure both sets have similar proportions:

``` r
set.seed(123)

# Stratify by the outcome variable
data_split <- initial_split(customer_data,
                            prop = 0.80,
                            strata = churn)  # ensures balanced split

train_data <- training(data_split)
test_data  <- testing(data_split)

# Verify churn rate is similar in both sets
train_data |> count(churn) |> mutate(pct = n / sum(n))
test_data  |> count(churn) |> mutate(pct = n / sum(n))
```

## The full tidymodels workflow

```{mermaid}
%%| echo: false
flowchart LR
  A[Marketing problem] --> B[Define outcome]
  B --> C[Split data\nrsample]
  C --> D[Preprocess\nrecipes]
  D --> E[Specify model\nparsnip]
  E --> F[Build workflow\nworkflows]
  F --> G[Evaluate\nyardstick]
  G --> H[Tune\ntune]
  H --> I[Final fit\non test set]
```

::: callout-note
We will build each step of this workflow systematically across the course. Today is the overview — by Week 10, this will feel natural.
:::

## Putting it together — a first taste

``` r
library(tidymodels)
set.seed(123)

# 1. Split
split      <- initial_split(customer_data, prop = 0.8, strata = churn)
train_data <- training(split)
test_data  <- testing(split)

# 2. Specify model
lr_spec <- logistic_reg() |>
  set_engine("glm")

# 3. Fit
lr_fit <- lr_spec |>
  fit(churn ~ recency + frequency + tenure, data = train_data)

# 4. Predict on test data
predictions <- predict(lr_fit, test_data) |>
  bind_cols(test_data)

# 5. Evaluate
predictions |>
  metrics(truth = churn, estimate = .pred_class)
```

# Class Activities {background-color="#FFB81C"}

## Activity 1 — Supervised or unsupervised?

For each marketing scenario, identify: **(a)** supervised or unsupervised, and **(b)** if supervised, regression or classification.

1.  Predict whether a customer will respond to a direct mail coupon.
2.  Group customers based on their purchase history.
3.  Predict next quarter's revenue for each product category.
4.  Discover common themes across 10,000 product reviews.
5.  Predict whether a free-trial user will convert to paid.
6.  Reduce 50 survey items to a smaller set of dimensions.
7.  Predict a customer's next purchase amount.
8.  Identify which customers are behaviorally similar to your best customers.

## Activity 2 — Define the outcome

A retail company says:

> "We want to use machine learning to improve our marketing."

In small groups (10 minutes):

1.  Identify **three specific prediction problems** this company could tackle
2.  For each, define:
    - The **outcome variable** (what are we predicting, exactly?)
    - The **type** of problem (classification or regression)
    - What **data** would you need?
    - What **marketing action** would the prediction enable?

::: callout-tip
Be specific. "Improve marketing" is not a modeling problem. "Predict which customers will churn in the next 30 days" is.
:::

## Activity 3 — Data budget planning

You are building a churn prediction model for a telecom company.

- You have **50,000 customer records**
- **4%** of customers churned in the past year

Answer these questions:

1.  How many rows would be in your training set (80/20 split)?
2.  Why should you use **stratified** splitting here?
3.  How many churners would you expect in the test set with stratification?
4.  What would go wrong if you used the test set to help you choose between models?

# Week 1 Takeaways {background-color="#FFB81C"}

## Key concepts from today

1.  **Machine learning** learns patterns from data to predict or classify new cases — it is different from traditional rule-based programming.

2.  **Supervised learning** uses labeled data to predict a known outcome — either a number (regression) or a category (classification).

3.  **Unsupervised learning** finds structure in data without a known outcome — clustering groups similar observations, dimensionality reduction compresses features.

4.  **tidymodels** provides a consistent, tidy workflow for all modeling tasks in R — split, preprocess, specify, fit, evaluate.

5.  **The data budget** matters — we split data into training and testing sets and use the test set only once to get honest performance estimates.

## Coming up

::::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}
### For next class

Read:

- TMWR Chapter 5 (review)
- TMWR Chapter 8 — Workflow basics

Be ready to work with:

- `initial_split()`
- `recipe()` and `step_*()` functions
- `workflow()`
- Dummy variables and normalization
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
### Course project

Start thinking about:

- A marketing dataset you find interesting
- A prediction question you want to answer
- Possible outcome variables

Good sources:

- Kaggle marketing datasets
- UCI Machine Learning Repository
- Your own work experience
:::
:::::

## Resources

| Resource | Link |
|----|----|
| Tidy Modeling with R (free online) | [tmwr.org](https://www.tmwr.org) |
| tidymodels official site | [tidymodels.org](https://www.tidymodels.org) |
| tidymodels Get Started | [tidymodels.org/start](https://www.tidymodels.org/start/) |
| tidyclust (unsupervised) | [tidyclust.tidymodels.org](https://tidyclust.tidymodels.org) |
| R for Data Science (free online) | [r4ds.hadley.nz](https://r4ds.hadley.nz) |

::: callout-note
All textbook readings are free online. You do not need to purchase anything for this course.
:::
